name: Cellulo
short_description: A platform of low-cost, palm-sized tangible robots that localize on printed paper via a microdot pattern and provide haptic feedback for hands-on learning.
long_description: Cellulo is a research platform of handheld, holonomic tangible robots developed at EPFL's CHILI lab that move across ordinary paper activity sheets, localizing with sub-millimeter accuracy by reading a printed optical microdot pattern through a downward-facing camera. Each robot offers haptic feedback and omnidirectional motion driven by magnet-assisted ball drives, letting groups of robots make abstract concepts (orbital mechanics, molecular vibration, handwriting) physically graspable in classrooms and rehabilitation. As a physical AI artifact it is significant as a tangible, embodied interface that blends robots into the everyday material ecology of paper and tablets rather than as an autonomous agent, foregrounding manipulation and haptics over machine intelligence.
ai_note: Cellulo is a tangible-robotics technique: camera-based microdot localization, holonomic ball-drive actuation, and haptic feedback, with no AI/ML model running inside the robots.

images.caption: Cellulo handheld tangible robots on a printed paper activity sheet, showing the palm-sized form and illuminated buttons.
images.alt_text: Several small rounded palm-sized robots with illuminated top buttons sitting on a printed paper sheet, held and moved by a person's hands.

sources.title: Cellulo: Versatile Handheld Robots for Education
sources.publisher: ACM/IEEE (Proceedings of the 2017 ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction)

sources.title: Cellulo Introduces Classrooms to Tangible Learning With Robots
